desc: The native "ladybug." Orange with black spots and converging white thoracic lines. Major aphid predator. Aggregates by millions in mountain canyons in winter.

fm.Color: Orange with 13 black spots; white converging thoracic lines
fm.Behavior: Aggregates in millions in mountain canyons for winter dormancy; important aphid predator
fm.Season: Year-round; disperses from mountain aggregations in spring

hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Preys on aphid. Canyon habitat.

desc: Seven-spotted ladybug — introduced from Europe as biocontrol agent, now outcompeting native ladybugs across NA. Larger than most native coccinellids. Diagnostic seven black spots on red elytra. Voracious aphid predator. Controversial — displaces native species.

fm.Behavior: Voracious aphid predator

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Aphid specialist.

desc: Invasive ladybug with extremely variable color and spot patterns (from red with many spots to black with two red spots). Aggregates in buildings in fall by the thousands. Bites, releases foul-smelling hemolymph. Outcompeting and displacing native ladybugs across NA.

fm.Color: Highly variable — orange to red with 0–22 spots
fm.Behavior: Aggregates in buildings; bites when handled; bad taste/smell; displacing natives
fm.Diet: Aphids, but also native ladybug larvae

hp: Introduced species. Beetle associate.

desc: Large (25mm), spectacular scarab attracted to lights on summer nights. Bold white stripes on brown elytra. Produces loud hissing/squeaking by forcing air through spiracles — startling when first heard. Males with enormous lamellate antennae for detecting female pheromones.

fm.Color: Brown with 10 bold white longitudinal stripes
fm.Behavior: Attracted to lights; hisses/squeaks when handled; strong clumsy flier; males have huge lamellate antennae
fm.Habitat: Sandy soil (larvae are root grubs); urban lights at night
fm.Diet: Larvae: plant roots. Adults: do not feed.
fm.Season: Jun–Aug; adults to lights summer nights

hp: Root-feeding grub in soil — adults attracted to lights in summer. Large (25mm) scarab with striped elytra.

desc: The largest beetle in western North America (males to 65mm including horn). Males with spectacular upward-curving thoracic horn. Extremely rare in LA County — restricted to mountain oak woodlands above 4,000 ft. Larvae develop in rotting wood for 2-3 years.

fm.Behavior: ♂ uses horn for fighting over ♀; nocturnal; attracted to lights; larva in decaying wood 2+ years
fm.Diet: Larvae: rotting wood. Adults: tree sap, fruit.

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Green fig beetle — the large, iridescent green scarab that bumbles loudly and clumsily through LA backyards every summer. A SoCal classic — arguably the most recognized insect in Los Angeles. Larvae are C-shaped grubs in compost. Adults feed on soft ripe fruit. Impressive buzz in flight.

fm.Color: Bright metallic green dorsally; iridescent green ventrally
fm.Behavior: Loud clumsy flight; bumbles into things; attracted to ripe fruit; larvae are "crawly backs" in compost
fm.Habitat: Urban gardens, orchards — anywhere with ripe fruit or compost
fm.Diet: Adults: ripe fruit, sap. Larvae: decomposing organic matter.

hp: Larvae (grubs) in compost. Adults on soft ripe fruit — figs, peaches, grapes. Loud buzzy flight. Iconic SoCal summer beetle.

desc: Green June beetle — very similar to the Figeater (C. mutabilis) but slightly different distribution. Difficult to distinguish without examination of tarsal claws. May hybridize with C. mutabilis where ranges overlap. Same loud, bumbling flight and iridescent green body.

fm.Behavior: Like Figeater

hp: Green June beetle — larvae (grubs) in compost and turf. Adults on ripe fruit. Very similar to Figeater. Eastern species at range edge.

desc: Massive (40-55mm) dark brown cerambycid. Males fly to lights on warm summer nights — alarming but harmless. Larvae bore in living tree roots for 3-5 years, potentially killing trees. Among the largest beetles regularly encountered in LA. Powerful mandibles.

fm.Color: Dark brown to black; robust; saw-toothed antennae
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; males fly to lights; females sluggish; larvae in tree roots 3–5 years
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, orchards, urban near trees
fm.Diet: Larvae: live tree roots (can damage orchards). Adults: do not feed.

hp: Largest cerambycid in western NA — males to 60mm (Linsley 1962). Larvae bore in dead/dying tree roots 3-5 yrs. Adults at lights.

desc: Stunning black-and-white banded longhorn, widely considered one of the most beautiful beetles in North America. Associated with hardwoods, especially alder. Larvae in dead wood. The dramatic zebra-like banding is unmistakable. Uncommon — a prized sighting.

fm.Color: White/gray with black bands; long antennae banded black and white
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; distinctive color makes it unmistakable
fm.Habitat: Riparian with alder, willow, ash; mountain canyons

hp: On alder (alnus) in riparian habitat. Associated with White Alder. Cerambycidae.

desc: Golden buprestid — brilliant metallic green and gold jewel beetle. One of the flashiest beetles in North America. Larvae bore in dead conifers for years — can emerge from milled lumber decades later. Extremely hard-bodied — iridescent structural coloring never fades, even in museum specimens.

fm.Color: Brilliant metallic green with gold/copper margins
fm.Behavior: Fast flier in sunny weather; attracted to freshly cut or fire-damaged wood; larvae in dead conifers
fm.Habitat: Coniferous forest, lumber yards, buildings with untreated wood
fm.Diet: Larvae: dead conifer wood. Adults: flower pollen.

hp: Larvae in dead wood — decomposer. In conifer forests. Associated with Ash.

desc: Flat-headed appletree borer — dark bronze-brown metallic buprestid. Major pest of fruit and shade trees — larvae bore flat-headed galleries under bark, girdling and killing branches. Prefers stressed trees. 'Flat-headed' refers to the larval body shape, not the adult.

fm.Behavior: Sunbathes on tree bark; larvae bore spiral galleries under bark

hp: Under bark — wood-boring community. On apple. Metallic coloring.

desc: Distinctive "click" mechanism — flips itself into air when on back. Large eyed-click beetle with false eyespots.

fm.Color: Black with two large white-rimmed false eyespots on pronotum
fm.Behavior: Click mechanism: thoracic spine snaps into groove, launching beetle into air with audible click
fm.Diet: Larvae (wireworms): plant roots. Adults: plant sap.

hp: Larvae predatory in decaying wood — eat wood-boring beetle larvae. Adults on dead logs. Click escape.

desc: Master blister beetle — spectacularly colored bright red-orange and black meloeid. Swarms on brittlebush (Encelia) flowers after desert spring rains. Contains cantharidin — handling causes skin blistering. One of the most visually stunning beetles in the Mojave-LA interface.

fm.Color: Bright red-orange head/thorax; metallic black elytra
fm.Behavior: Swarms on Encelia flowers in desert spring; bleeds cantharidin from joints if harassed (blistering agent)
fm.Habitat: Desert, coastal sage — on Encelia, Larrea
fm.Diet: Adults: flowers/pollen. Larvae: parasitic on ground bee nests.
fm.Season: Mar–May; mass emergence after desert rains

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Blister.

desc: ♀ is bioluminescent larva-like (neotenic). ♂ is typical beetle that flies to lights. ♀ glows green from lateral organs.

fm.Size: ♂: ½ in–⅔ in. ♀: 1–2.5 in (larval form; neotenic).
fm.Color: ♂: brown, typical beetle. ♀: wingless, larval form, glows green.
fm.Behavior: ♀ bioluminescent — glows green from lateral light organs; attracts ♂; predator of millipedes
fm.Season: Oct–Nov; ♂ to lights fall; ♀ glow on ground

hp: Bioluminescent — female glows green on hillsides at night to attract flying males. Larvae prey on millipedes.

desc: Blue death-feigning beetle — chalky blue-white darkling beetle that plays dead convincingly when disturbed. Desert species. Popular in vivarium hobby.

fm.Behavior: Plays dead when disturbed — very convincing

hp: Desert detritivore — nocturnal under rocks. Family Tenebrionidae. Desert specialist. Ironclad.

desc: Bright red-orange with black spots. Feeds on milkweed — sequesters cardiac glycosides for defense. Warning coloration.

fm.Color: Bright red-orange with 4 black spots (each eye is split by antenna base — hence "tetrophthalmus")
fm.Behavior: Feeds on milkweed; warning coloration (toxic); squeaks when handled
fm.Habitat: Anywhere Asclepias grows — gardens, roadsides, meadows

hp: On milkweed — sequesters cardiac glycosides. Sequesters cardiac glycosides for defense. Associated with Narrow-leaf Milkweed.

desc: Unusual ladybug: pale cream-tan with sparse dark spots, unlike the typical bold red-and-black pattern. Associated with pines and conifers. Feeds on conifer aphids. Distinguished from typical coccinellids by pale ground color and conifer habitat preference.

fm.Color: Pale cream/tan with sparse dark spots
fm.Behavior: Found on pine/fir needles; feeds on conifer aphids

hp: On Pinus (pines) — specialist aphid predator on conifer aphids. Pale cream ladybug — unusual coloring for a coccinellid.

desc: Arizona stag beetle — large beetle with males sporting huge mandibles used in combat for mates. Larvae develop in rotting oak wood for 1-2 years. Impressive when found.

fm.Size: 1–1.5 in (♂ with mandibles up to 2 in)
fm.Color: Dark brown to black; large mandibles (♂)
fm.Behavior: ♂ combat over ♀ using mandibles; nocturnal; to lights; larvae in rotting wood 2+ years
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, rotting stumps and logs
fm.Diet: Larvae: decaying oak wood. Adults: tree sap.

hp: Larvae feed on oak foliage in woodland. Associated with Coast Live Oak. On oaks (Quercus).

desc: Spectacular — metallic silver-green with green stripes. One of most beautiful NA beetles. Associated with juniper at elevation.

fm.Color: Brilliant metallic silver-green with green stripes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; feeds on juniper foliage; larvae in soil
fm.Habitat: Juniper/oak woodland above 5,000' (edge of range in LA mountains)

hp: Glorious scarab on Juniperus (juniper) in mountains. Metallic green with silver stripes. One of NA's most spectacular beetles.

desc: CA's diurnal "firefly" — does NOT glow. Dark with orange thorax. Feeds on tree sap and pollen. True bioluminescent fireflies are absent from most of SoCal.

fm.Color: Black elytra; orange/red thorax with dark center
fm.Behavior: Diurnal (unlike eastern fireflies); does not produce light; feeds on sap/pollen

hp: Day-active firefly relative (does not glow). Feeds on tree sap and pollen. True lampyrid beetle.

desc: Spectacularly metallic green, blue, and copper dung beetle. Males with prominent curved horn. At extreme western range edge in SoCal — primarily eastern/central NA. Any LA County record is noteworthy. One of the most beautiful beetles in North America.

fm.Color: Metallic green, blue, copper; ♂ with curved horn
fm.Behavior: Buries dung for larvae; tunneler (not roller)
fm.Habitat: Open areas with large mammal dung (range edge)

hp: On dung — nutrient recycler and parasite control. Scarabaeidae.

desc: Bright red, white, and blue-black patterned clerid. Predator of bark beetles — both adults and larvae hunt bark beetle larvae in their galleries. Beneficial forest insect. Named for preeminent 19th-century entomologist John Lawrence LeConte.

fm.Color: Bright red pronotum; blue-black checkered elytra
fm.Behavior: Hunts bark beetles on tree bark; both adults and larvae are predators
fm.Habitat: Pine and conifer forests; bark of infested trees
fm.Season: Year-round; peaks during bark beetle outbreaks

hp: Bark beetle predator — important forest biocontrol. Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: Tiny beetle that changes color — from metallic gold to red-orange when disturbed. Miniature jewel on morning glory leaves.

fm.Color: Metallic gold (at rest) → red-orange (disturbed); transparent margins
fm.Behavior: Color-changing — gold to red when stressed; on morning glory leaves; larvae carry fecal shield
fm.Habitat: Gardens — on Convolvulaceae (morning glory, sweet potato)

hp: On morning glory and bindweed (Convolvulaceae). Changes from gold to red-orange when disturbed. Leaf feeder.

desc: Yellow-green with 12 black spots (despite the name 'undecim' = eleven). Dual threat: adults damage cucurbit flowers and foliage; larvae ('southern corn rootworm') feed on corn and legume roots. Vectors bacterial wilt and mosaic viruses.

fm.Behavior: Feeds on flowers and leaves of many plants; transmits bacterial wilt
fm.Habitat: Agricultural fields, gardens — anywhere with crops

hp: Corn (Zea mays) associate — larvae feed on roots, silk, or kernels. On corn. Chrysomelidae.

desc: Tiny (4mm) but ecologically critical bark beetle. Mass attacks kill ponderosa and Coulter pines during drought stress. Creates diagnostic 'pitch tubes' (resin globules) on bark surface. Pheromone-coordinated mass attacks overwhelm tree defenses. Major forest mortality agent.

fm.Behavior: Mass pheromone-coordinated attacks on drought-stressed pines; creates pitch tubes

hp: In pine bark/wood — decomposer. Associated with Coulter Pine. On Pinus (pine). Forest (4mm).

desc: Common furniture beetle — the 'woodworm' whose larvae bore tiny round exit holes (1-2mm) in dry structural and furniture wood. Can cause significant damage to older buildings and antiques. Larvae develop for 3-5 years in seasoned timber. European introduction.

fm.Behavior: Larvae bore in dry wood for years; create <⅛ in round exit holes; "death watch" tapping

hp: Wood/stem borer. Beetle associate. Ant associate.

desc: Eucalyptus paropsis — invasive Australian leaf beetle defoliating eucalyptus. Both larvae and adults skeletonize leaves. Yellow-brown with dark spots. Established in SoCal.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Associated with Blue Gum.

desc: Orange-red ladybug entirely without spots. Native tropical/subtropical species. Important aphid predator. Distinguished from other ladybugs by total lack of spots.

fm.Color: Entirely spotless orange-red — no spots at all

hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Aphid specialist. Predatory.

desc: Extraordinary beetle whose interlocking exoskeletal joints make it nearly impossible to crush. Can survive being run over by a car. Engineers study its structure.

fm.Note: Nearly crush-proof — studied by materials engineers

hp: Ironclad exoskeleton — withstands forces 39,000x body weight (Rivera et al. 2020, Nature). Interlocking suture joints inspire engineering.

desc: Hairy darkling beetle — medium Eleodes densely covered in golden-brown pubescence. Desert and foothill trails. Classic headstand defense posture. Nocturnal.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Desert specialist. Densely hairy body.

desc: Sharp-tailed darkling beetle — common foothill trail Eleodes with pointed elytral tips. Nocturnal. Classic headstand posture spraying quinones when disturbed.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Nocturnal. Pointed-tail.

desc: Tiny rounded beetle whose larvae destroy wool, silk, feathers, and museum specimens. Adults feed on pollen at flowers. One of the most destructive household pests.
hp: Most destructive museum pest worldwide — larvae eat dried animal specimens, wool, silk (Pinniger 2001). Adults visit flowers. Tiny (2-3mm).

desc: Pacific soldier beetle — soft-bodied cantharid on flowers. Predatory on small insects. Soft leathery wing covers. Common on chaparral flowers in spring.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Chaparral habitat. Family Cantharidae. Soft-bodied cantharid on flowers. Predatory on small insects.

desc: Tiny cream ladybug with 20 dark spots. Unusual among ladybugs — feeds on powdery mildew fungus rather than aphids. Beneficial fungus predator.

fm.Color: Cream with 20 brown spots; eats mildew

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Preys on aphid.

desc: Orange chrysomelid with three black stripes. On Solanaceae (nightshade family). Larvae carry fecal shields on their backs for defense.

fm.Behavior: Larvae carry fecal shield on back

hp: Specialist on Solanaceae — nightshade, Datura, eggplant, potato. Larvae carry fecal shield for protection.

desc: Eucalyptus tortoise beetle — invasive Australian chrysomelid. Flat rounded shape. Both adults and larvae defoliate eucalyptus. Arrived in SoCal ~2003. Expanding.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Invasive Australian chrysomelid. Flat rounded shape. Both ad.

desc: Red-necked ground beetle — small carabid with dark elytra and reddish pronotum. Introduced from Europe. Very common under rocks and debris in gardens. Nocturnal predator.
hp: Ground-hunting predator. Beneficial in gardens.

desc: Spectacular red and metallic blue-black clerid. Adults on flowers. Larvae invade bee nests and eat bee larvae. Beautiful but destructive to native bees.

fm.Behavior: Larvae eat bee larvae in nests

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Cleridae.

desc: Introduced Australian chrysomelid defoliating eucalyptus. Both adults and larvae feed on leaves. Recent pest in SoCal.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Introduced Australian chrysomelid defoliating eucalyptus. Bo.

desc: Introduced Australian ladybug whose larvae are covered in white waxy filaments mimicking mealybugs. Larvae infiltrate mealybug colonies to feed. Important biocontrol.

fm.Color: Dark with orange head; larvae mimic mealybugs

hp: Introduced for biological pest control. Preys on mealybug.

desc: Six-spotted flower longhorn — colorful cerambycid with six pale spots on dark elytra. Visits flowers in chaparral. Larvae in dead conifer and hardwood branches.

fm.Color: Dark with 6 pale spots; on flowers

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Chaparral habitat. Family Cerambycidae. Common species.

desc: Tan scarab whose larvae (white grubs) damage turf roots. Adults at lights on summer nights. Can emerge in enormous numbers.

fm.Damage: White grub larvae destroy turf roots

hp: Turf pest — larvae (white grubs) feed on grass roots, damaging lawns and turf.

desc: Fuller rose beetle — gray-brown weevil reproducing entirely by parthenogenesis (all female, no males). Flightless. Pest of citrus and ornamentals — notches leaf margins.
hp: Root weevil on Citrus and Rosa. Parthenogenetic — all-female. Nocturnal leaf-notching adult.

desc: Eared blister beetle — spectacular metallic green blister beetle with reddish head. Contains cantharidin — causes severe skin blistering. On desert flowers. Handle with caution.

fm.Color: Metallic green body + red head — striking

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Spectacular. Metallic coloring.

desc: Ladybug with two distinct color forms: gray with black spots OR all-black with two red spots. Important predator of psyllids and aphids. Native.

fm.Color: Two forms: gray+spots OR black+2 red spots

hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Associated with Ash.

desc: Blue milkweed beetle — stunning metallic cobalt blue chrysomelid. On milkweed (Asclepias). Sequesters milkweed toxins for defense. One of the most beautiful beetles in California.
hp: Blue milkweed beetle — sequesters cardenolides from Asclepias making it toxic to predators (Dobler et al. 2012, Mol Ecol). Iridescent cobalt blue.

desc: Native spotless orange ladybug. Once common, now declining due to competition from introduced lady beetles. Important conservation indicator species.
hp: Aphid predator on native plants. Once common — declining due to invasive Asian lady beetle competition.

desc: Tiny red-and-black ladybug introduced from Australia in 1888 to control cottony cushion scale — saved the California citrus industry. First successful classical biocontrol.
hp: Vedalia beetle — first successful classical biocontrol in history (1888). Associated with Sweet Orange.

desc: Pleated ironclad beetle — close relative of the diabolical ironclad. Interlocking exoskeletal joints resist crushing. Under oak bark. Desert and foothill habitats.
hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. Desert specialist.

desc: Pink glowworm — SoCal's equivalent of a firefly. Flightless larviform females glow bright pink-green at night on hillsides. Males winged but non-luminous. Bioluminescent courtship unique to this genus.

fm.Behavior: ♀ glows on ground fall evenings; ♂ flies to find glowing ♀

hp: Bioluminescent — wingless larviform females glow pink-green on hillsides at dusk. Males fly to lights. Predatory larvae.

desc: Large black rove beetle that raises tail scorpion-like when threatened. Introduced from Europe. Opens mandibles aggressively. Can bite. Nocturnal predator.

fm.Color: Shiny black; raises tail like scorpion
fm.Behavior: Scorpion-like threat display; can bite

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Nocturnal. Devil's.

desc: Metallic green predator with three pale elytral bands forming an S-pattern. Hunts on stream margins and moist sand. Fast runner; flies when disturbed. A visual highlight of riparian habitats.

fm.Behavior: Fast runner; active predator

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Riparian habitat.

desc: Yellow-green chrysomelid with three bold black elytral stripes. Feeds on cucurbit crops and native Cucurbita. Vectors bacterial wilt (Erwinia tracheiphila) and squash mosaic virus. Distinguished from Banded Cucumber Beetle by continuous (not banded) stripes. Year-round in LA gardens.

fm.Behavior: Feeds on cucurbit flowers and leaves; disease vector

hp: Cucurbit specialist — on squash, pumpkin, or gourd (Cucurbita).

desc: Major stored-product pest. Tiny (3-4mm) red-brown beetle infesting flour, cereal, and grain. 'Confused' because easily confused with the closely related T. castaneum. Also a model organism in ecology and evolutionary biology research — extensively studied for 100+ years.

fm.Behavior: Infests stored grain/flour; model organism in ecology

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Tenebrionidae.

desc: Tiny (2mm) black chrysomelid devastating to Brassicaceae (mustard family) seedlings. Enlarged hind femora enable powerful jumping when disturbed. Characteristic 'shot-hole' feeding damage — tiny round holes in leaves. Can destroy young crops rapidly.

fm.Behavior: Massive damage to Brassicaceae seedlings; jumps when disturbed

hp: Visual jumping spider — stalks and pounces on prey. Chrysomelidae.

desc: Pale tan scarab with characteristically long legs swarming on roses and light-colored flowers in spring. Larvae are white grubs in turf. Adults can be toxic to birds and chickens if consumed in quantity. A familiar spring garden nuisance.

fm.Behavior: Swarms roses and ornamental flowers; clumsy flier

hp: On Rosa (roses) — garden and wild species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Federally Threatened. Green with red wing margins. Obligate on elderberry. Central Valley species at range edge in LA.

fm.Color: Metallic green with red/orange wing margins
fm.Behavior: Adults on elderberry flowers; larvae bore in elderberry stems 1–2 years
fm.Diet: Larvae: elderberry pith. Adults: elderberry flowers

hp: Valley elderberry longhorn beetle. FT under ESA (45 FR 52803, 1980). Larvae in Sambucus stems. Riparian habitat loss.

desc: Large, metallic ground beetle. Caterpillar hunter — climbs trees to eat tent caterpillars. Important biocontrol.

fm.Color: Black with green/bronze metallic sheen; robust
fm.Behavior: Climbs trees to hunt caterpillars; nocturnal; aggressive predator
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, orchards — wherever caterpillar outbreaks occur
fm.Diet: Predator — caterpillars (major biocontrol of tent caterpillars)

hp: Biological control agent — predator of caterpillars. Preys on caterpillar. Metallic coloring.

desc: Large, hairy rove beetle attracted to carrion. Short wing covers expose flexible abdomen. Important forensic indicator.

fm.Color: Black with gray hairy bands; short wing covers
fm.Behavior: At carrion; raises tail scorpion-like; predator of maggots; forensic indicator

hp: On carrion — essential decomposer. Forensically important. Ant associate.

desc: Fuzzy scarab with dense golden pile convincingly mimicking a bumblebee. Buzzy, erratic flight between flowers and ripe fruit. Smaller and fuzzier than the Figeater. Larvae in decaying organic matter. Adults attracted to ripe figs and stone fruit.

fm.Behavior: Buzzy flight among flowers; visits ripe fruit; bumblebee mimic
fm.Habitat: Gardens, orchards, meadows — flowers and fruit

hp: Mimicry — Batesian or aggressive. Beetle associate.

desc: Agave snout weevil — large black weevil (15mm) that kills agave and yucca by boring into the plant's heart. Major pest of century plants — infested agave collapses suddenly. Larvae feed communally in the decaying base. Devastating to ornamental and native agave plantings.

fm.Behavior: Bores into agave/yucca heart; can kill mature plants

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Chaparral Yucca.

desc: Massive longhorn beetle — up to 3 in. Larvae in palo verde roots for years. Adults fly to lights on summer nights. Desert species reaching LA.

fm.Color: Dark brown; robust; long antennae; spined thorax
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flies to lights summer; larvae in palo verde roots 3+ years; harmless
fm.Habitat: Desert — wherever palo verde trees grow
fm.Diet: Larvae: palo verde roots. Adults: do not feed
fm.Season: Jun–Aug; adults at lights summer nights

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Among the largest.

desc: Desert stink beetle — common darkling with diagnostic lateral thoracic spines (armata = armed). Famous headstand defense posture — tips abdomen skyward and sprays foul-smelling quinone secretions. Flightless. Iconic desert insect. The model for the 'stinkbug' name in the Southwest.

fm.Behavior: Headstand defense; quinone spray; flightless; trails at dusk

hp: Desert stink beetle — headstand sprays p-benzoquinones at predators (Tschinkel 1975, J Insect Physiol). Iconic desert beetle.

desc: Beautiful golden-yellow scarab attracted to lights on warm nights. Larvae feed on roots in soil for 2-3 years. One of the most attractive scarabs.

fm.Shape: Golden-yellow scarab

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Scarabaeidae.

desc: California fungus beetle — black erotylid with bright red-orange spots. Found on bracket fungi and shelf mushrooms on dead trees. Distinctive coloring warns of distastefulness.

fm.Shape: Black beetle with red-orange spots

hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi and fungal hyphae. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Erotylidae.

desc: Brilliant metallic green meloeid that swarms on desert flowers after spring rains. Contains cantharidin, causing blistering on skin contact. Named for Thomas Say, father of American entomology. Hypermetamorphic — larvae parasitize ground-nesting bee nests.

fm.Habitat: Swarms on desert flowers after rain

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Metallic coloring.

desc: Metallic violet-blue meloeid with distinctive iridescent sheen. Contains cantharidin. Hypermetamorphic — first-instar larvae (triungulins) are mobile, climbing flowers to hitchhike on bees back to nests where they consume bee provisions.

fm.Shape: Metallic violet-blue blister beetle

hp: Flower visitor. Contains cantharidin. Larvae parasitize bee nests. Meloidae.

desc: Mountain bark beetle — tiny bothriderid found under bark of dead oaks and conifers. Predatory on wood-boring beetle larvae — beneficial natural enemy. Flat body adapted for bark crevice habitat. Mountain and foothill dead-wood communities. Easily overlooked.

fm.Shape: Tiny bark beetle found under bark of dead oaks and conifers

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Large brown longhorn of conifer forests. One of the largest cerambycids in LA County. Larvae bore in dead pine and fir roots for several years. Mountain habitats.

fm.Habitat: Large brown longhorn of conifer forests

hp: Largest longhorn in Pinus forests — to 50mm (Linsley 1962). Larvae in dead pine wood. Declining old-growth associate.

desc: Dark flightless weevil with roughened elytra. Major pest of ornamental plants. Larvae devastate roots of rhododendrons, yews, and strawberries. Nocturnal. Introduced from Europe.
hp: Nocturnal — attracted to lights. On strawberr.

desc: Desert ironclad blister beetle — bizarre inflated blue-black meloeid of Mojave Desert margins. Walks slowly with swollen abdomen held high. Contains cantharidin. Resembles no other beetle — inflated shape unique among NA beetles. On desert flowers after rain.

fm.Shape: Inflated blue-black blister beetle of Mojave margins

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat.

desc: Striking bright red flattened beetle under bark of dead trees. Larvae are freeze-tolerant to -58°F using antifreeze proteins — studied by cryobiologists.

fm.Shape: Bright red flattened beetle under bark of dead trees

hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Preys on bark beetle.

desc: Elongated (15mm) brentid weevil with snout longer than body. Breeds in hardwood logs. Males fight over females using their elongated snouts as weapons, jousting head-to-head on log surfaces. One of the most morphologically distinctive weevils in LA County.

fm.Shape: Elongate weevil with snout longer than body

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Elongated (15mm).

desc: Mountain stream beetle — ancient relict beetle of cold, fast mountain streams. Poor swimmer — clings to rocks in rapids using tarsal claws. Family Amphizoidae has only 5 species worldwide, all in western NA and eastern Asia. A living fossil — unchanged for millions of years.

fm.Habitat: Ancient relict beetle of cold mountain streams

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Amphizoidae.

desc: Black-and-yellow chrysomelid on willows and poplars. Both larvae and adults skeletonize leaves, sometimes causing severe defoliation along riparian corridors. Distinctive yellow elytra with variable black markings. Common in LA's riparian habitats.

fm.Shape: Black and yellow leaf beetle on willows and poplars

hp: On Salix (willows) and Populus (cottonwoods) in riparian habitat. Larvae and adults skeletonize leaves. Can defoliate riparian corridors.

desc: Cactus longhorn beetle — large (30mm), entirely black, flightless cerambycid specialized on Opuntia and Cylindropuntia cacti. Larvae bore through cactus pads causing segments to collapse. Adults nocturnal — found walking on cactus at night. Fused elytra. One of LA's most distinctive beetles.

fm.Shape: Flightless black longhorn on prickly pear and cholla

hp: Wood/stem borer. Associated with Coastal Prickly Pear. Beetle associate.

desc: Green chrysomelid with cream elytral stripes. On cucurbit flowers. Can vector bacterial wilt of cucumbers. Common in summer vegetable gardens.

fm.Habitat: Found on cucurbit flowers in summer gardens

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Invasive metallic green jewel beetle devastating ash trees across eastern NA. Not yet confirmed in LA County but actively monitored by CDFA/USDA. If established, would threaten all native and ornamental Fraxinus. D-shaped exit holes diagnostic. USDA quarantine pest.
hp: Emerald ash borer — has killed >100 million ash trees in eastern NA (Herms & McCullough 2014, Ann Rev Entomol). Not yet in LA. USDA quarantine.

desc: Twelve-spotted asparagus beetle — orange chrysomelid with 12 black spots. Pest of asparagus along with the related asparagus beetle. Introduced from Europe. Adults and larvae on asparagus ferns.
hp: Asparagus pest — larvae and adults feed on asparagus fern. European introduction.

desc: Shiny jet-black ladybug with two bold red spots. Specialist predator of armored scale insects. Important biological control agent — commercially used in citrus IPM. Distinguished from other dark ladybugs by the exactly two red spots.

fm.Shape: Shiny black with two red spots

hp: Citrus; predatory; biocontrol agent. On Citrus (citrus).

desc: Collared flea beetle — tiny iridescent blue-green chrysomelid that jumps when disturbed. On evening primrose (Oenothera) and willowherb (Epilobium). Enlarged hind femora for jumping. Can cause significant defoliation when abundant. Distinguished from A. bimarginata by host plant.

fm.Shape: Tiny iridescent blue-green beetle that jumps when disturbed

hp: Flea beetle on Oenothera (evening primrose) and Epilobium. Specialist Onagraceae feeder.

desc: Giant diving beetle — large aquatic beetle to 35mm. Streamlined body with fringed swimming legs. Both larvae and adults are voracious predators — hunting tadpoles, aquatic insects, and small fish. Breathes by trapping air under elytra. One of LA's largest aquatic insects.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Predatory (35mm).

desc: Remarkable social beetle living in family groups in rotting logs. Parents and larvae communicate by stridulation (squeaking). Adults chew wood, feed pre-digested wood pulp to larvae. Both parents provide biparental care — rare among beetles. At western range limit.

fm.Shape: Social beetle living in rotting logs

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Passalidae.

desc: Green beetle whose larva is the billion-dollar agricultural pest of corn. Larvae feed on corn roots, causing lodging (plants fall over). Adults eat corn silk and pollen. Evolved resistance to crop rotation and multiple insecticides. Economic impact enormous.

fm.Shape: Green beetle — larva is billion-dollar crop pest

hp: Corn rootworm — $1B+ annual US crop losses (Gray et al. 2009, J Integrated Pest Mgmt). Larvae destroy corn root systems.

desc: Grapevine hoplia — small iridescent scarab with silvery-green males. Swarms on roses and light-colored flowers in spring, causing petal damage. 'Callipyge' means 'beautiful buttocks' — referring to the rounded posterior. Larvae are white grubs in soil. Can defoliate roses.

fm.Shape: Small iridescent scarab — males silver-green

hp: Viticulture associate — on grapevines (Vitis). Vineyard pest or resident. Associated with Rose.

desc: Hairy-rump scarab — brown scarab with white elytral stripes. Produces a remarkable hissing/squeaking sound by forcing air through abdominal spiracles when handled. Mountain areas. Larvae feed on roots underground for 2-3 years. Males attracted to lights in summer.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Scarabaeidae.

desc: Small dark tiger beetle with white elytral dots. Extremely fast predator of open ground — can run at 5.6 mph (scaled to body size, one of the fastest animals on Earth). Larvae dig vertical burrow traps, ambushing passing insects. Sandy open habitats.

fm.Shape: Small dark tiger beetle with white spots

hp: Open ground predator. Tiger beetle — runs at ~120 body lengths/sec (Kamoun & Hogenhout 1999). Larval pit-trap ambusher.

desc: Green tiger beetle of sandy creek banks and river margins. Among the fastest running insects relative to body size — up to 5.6 mph (≈120 body lengths/sec). Huge eyes for tracking prey at speed. Must stop periodically because running too fast temporarily blinds it.

fm.Behavior: Extremely fast runner; ambush from burrow as larva

hp: Sandy creek banks. Among fastest running insects — 5.6 mph (Cole 1942, J Kansas Ent Soc). Visually tracks prey at speed.

desc: Long-lipped tiger beetle — dark bronze tiger beetle of mountain trails and clearings. Active predator running down prey on hot sunny days. Excellent vision.

fm.Habitat: Dark bronze tiger beetle of mountain trails

hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Mountain habitat.

desc: Small (6mm) red-orange and black chrysomelid. Serious pest of asparagus — adults and larvae feed on spears and fern. European introduction. Both adults and larvae skeletonize fronds. Can be controlled with native parasitoid wasps and hand-picking.
hp: Parasitoid — biological control agent. Beetle associate.

desc: Gold-spotted brown rove beetle — large predatory staphylinid with characteristic short elytra exposing abdominal segments. Gold-spotted pattern distinctive. Hunts caterpillars and soft-bodied grubs in leaf litter. Raises abdomen like a scorpion when threatened but cannot sting.

fm.Shape: Large predatory rove beetle with short elytra

hp: Caterpillar predator — hunts moth and butterfly larvae. Preys on caterpillar. Predatory.

desc: Arizona net-winged beetle — bright orange with distinctively expanded, net-veined elytra. Toxic — contains lycid acids. Model species in a Müllerian mimicry ring with other orange-and-black beetles. Found on flowers and tree trunks. Predators learn to avoid the color pattern.

fm.Shape: Orange with expanded net-veined elytra

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Bee mimic.

desc: Lewis's soldier beetle — soft-bodied yellow-orange cantharid extremely abundant on flowers in fall. Both predator and pollinator — eats small insects on flower heads while incidentally transferring pollen. Common on buckwheat and goldenrod. One of the most abundant fall beetles.

fm.Shape: Soft-bodied yellow-orange beetle abundant on flowers

hp: Buckwheat; pollinator; predatory. Associated with California Buckwheat.

desc: Spotted grapevine beetle — large golden-tan scarab with black spots. Larvae develop in rotting wood (not living roots). Adults feed on grape and Virginia creeper leaves. At western range edge in SoCal. Impressive size and golden coloring make it unmistakable.

fm.Shape: Large golden-tan scarab with black spots

hp: Viticulture associate — on grapevines (Vitis). Vineyard pest or resident.

desc: Yellow-green chrysomelid defoliating elm trees. European introduction. Both adults and larvae feed on leaves — adults create shot-holes, larvae skeletonize undersides. Can cause complete defoliation of urban elms. Significant pest of LA's street trees.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Urban habitat.

desc: Punctured rain beetle — large, shiny black flightless scarab emerging only during fall/winter rains. Larvae feed on roots deep underground for 10-13 years before pupating — among the longest-lived beetle larvae. Males stumble across ground seeking buried females. Ancient lineage.

fm.Shape: Flightless scarab emerging only during fall rains

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Scarabaeidae.

desc: Dried-fruit beetle — tiny nitidulid infesting stored dried fruit, nuts, dates, and fermenting produce. Adults fly to fermenting odors. Major post-harvest pest causing millions in crop losses. Cosmopolitan — found wherever dried fruit is stored. Club-shaped antennae.
hp: Stored product pest of dried foods and grain. Tiny. Dried Fruit.

desc: Small dark dermestid. Larvae more damaging than the varied carpet beetle — feed on wool, silk, leather, museum specimens, and dried animal products. Larvae distinctive: elongated with a long tail tuft of hairs. Very common household pest.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Dermestidae.

desc: Tiny brown anobiid infesting an extraordinary range of dried materials: foods, spices, pharmaceuticals, leather, books, museum specimens. Eats almost anything organic. Named 'paniceum' (bread) but diet far broader. One of the most polyphagous stored-product pests.
hp: Stored product pest of dried foods and grain. Anobiidae.

desc: Pressed cactus longhorn — large flightless black cerambycid specializing on prickly pear and cholla cactus. Fused elytra (cannot fly). Larvae bore through cactus pads causing them to collapse. Nocturnal — found walking on cactus at night. Can stridulate when handled.

fm.Shape: Flightless black longhorn on prickly pear and cholla

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.)

desc: Simple tumble-bug — small black dung beetle that sculpts dung into balls and rolls them away for burial and egg-laying. Important nutrient recycler and parasite control agent — buries dung, reducing fly breeding habitat. Found in grasslands and rangeland with herbivore dung.

fm.Shape: Small black dung beetle rolling balls of dung

hp: Herbivore on host plants.

desc: California five-spined engraver — bark beetle of pines. Galleries create characteristic fan-shaped pattern under bark. Pheromone-mediated mass attacks — pioneering males release attractant that recruits hundreds more beetles to overwhelm tree defenses. Major pine forest mortality agent.

fm.Shape: Bark beetle of pines

hp: In Pinus (pine) bark — mass attacks kill stressed trees. Forest mortality agent.

desc: Common whirligig — shiny black beetle spinning in frantic circles on water surface. Split compound eyes see above and below water simultaneously. Uses surface tension to skate. Secretes defensive nausea-inducing compound. Extremely fast — one of the fastest aquatic insects.

fm.Shape: Shiny black beetle spinning on water surface

hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Gyrinidae.

desc: Western diving beetle — large (30mm) streamlined aquatic predator. Hunts tadpoles, small fish, and other aquatic invertebrates. Breathes air trapped under elytra — surfaces tail-first to replenish. Powerful swimmer using fringed hind legs as oars. Flies between water bodies at night.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Beetle associate. Aquatic.

desc: Alder flea beetle — tiny metallic blue-green chrysomelid that jumps powerfully when disturbed. Skeletonizes willow and alder leaves. Gregarious — large numbers can cause significant defoliation along riparian corridors. Enlarged hind femora contain the jumping muscle. Native.

fm.Shape: Tiny metallic blue-green beetle jumping when disturbed

hp: Willow; alder. Riparian. Associated with Red Willow and White Alder.

desc: Knaus's cactus weevil — small gray-brown weevil specializing on prickly pear cactus. Larvae bore into cactus pads, creating entry points for bacterial rot. Important cactus herbivore — can cause significant pad damage. Desert and desert-edge habitats throughout SoCal.

fm.Shape: Small weevil on prickly pear cactus

hp: Wood/stem borer. Associated with Coastal Prickly Pear. Desert habitat.

desc: Humped jewel beetle — small metallic buprestid with yellow markings and distinctive humped body profile. Adults visit flowers in chaparral. Larvae bore in dead branches of native shrubs. Part of the huge western Acmaeodera radiation — over 100 species in NA.

fm.Shape: Small jewel beetle with yellow markings

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Chaparral habitat. Metallic. Family Buprestidae. Larvae bore in dead branches of native shrubs.

desc: Sprays boiling hot benzoquinone from abdominal tip at 100°C with audible pop. The chemical reaction occurs in an explosion chamber — hydrogen peroxide + hydroquinone catalyzed by enzymes. Can fire 500+ times. One of the most remarkable chemical defenses in nature.

fm.Shape: Sprays boiling hot quinone from abdomen at 100°C with audible pop

hp: Bombardier beetle — explosive 100°C quinone spray at 500+ pulses/sec (Eisner et al. 1977, Science). Most remarkable chemical defense in insects.

desc: Parenthesis ladybug — orange ladybug with distinctive black parenthesis-shaped marks flanking the pronotum. Native species. Important aphid predator on crops and in gardens. Distinguished from other Hippodamia by the diagnostic parenthesis mark. Common in grassland and gardens.

fm.Shape: Orange ladybug with parenthesis-shaped marks on pronotum

hp: Predatory. Gardens and urban areas. Aphid predator — biocontrol.

desc: Tiny (2-3mm) brown anobiid closely resembling the drugstore beetle. Originally a pest of stored tobacco, now infests spices, dried foods, paprika, and dried flowers. Among the most destructive pantry pests. Distinguished from drugstore beetle by serrate antennae.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). On tobacco.

desc: Rusty grain beetle — extremely small (1.5-2mm) flat beetle in stored grain, flour, and cereal products. One of the smallest beetles commonly encountered in homes and food storage. Flat body allows entry through tiny gaps in packaging. Cosmopolitan pest.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Laemophloeidae.

desc: Carrion-feeding beetle used by museums to clean bones. Also infests stored products. Important forensic indicator.
hp: Stored product pest of dried foods and grain. On carrion.

desc: Red-and-black clerid with wasp-like coloring. Predatory on bark beetle larvae in conifer bark. Important natural biocontrol agent — can significantly reduce bark beetle outbreaks in pine forests. Adults also found on flowers.

fm.Shape: Red and black predatory beetle hunting bark beetles on conifers

hp: Biocontrol agent; bark beetle predator; pine. Associated with Coulter Pine.

desc: Large black ground beetle with massive mandibles. Burrows in garden soil. Nocturnal predator of larvae and slugs.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Unusual plant-eating ladybug (most coccinellids are predators). Orange with eight spots per elytron. Major pest of snap and lima beans. Larvae and adults skeletonize leaves from below. Distinguished from beneficial ladybugs by plant-feeding habit.

fm.Color: Orange with 16 black spots — resembles ladybug
fm.Behavior: Herbivorous — unusual for a ladybug

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Beetle associate. Coccinellidae.

desc: Iridescent green-copper scarab. Not yet established in LA but under quarantine surveillance. Devastating turf pest.

fm.Origin: Japanese — under active quarantine in CA

hp: Quarantine pest — not established in LA. Larvae destroy turf; adults defoliate 300+ plant species.

desc: Sculptured pine buprestid — large (25mm) dark metallic jewel beetle with deeply sculptured elytral texture. Larvae bore in dead ponderosa and other pines. Adults found on felled logs and stumps. One of the largest buprestids in western NA. Mountain conifer forests.

fm.Habitat: Pine forests — dead and dying trees

hp: In pine bark/wood — decomposer. On Pinus (pine). Montane (25mm).

desc: Black flightless beetle that raises abdomen (headstanding) when threatened. Desert darkling — common on trails.

fm.Color: Black; smooth or rough depending on species
fm.Defense: Headstand display — sprays quinones from abdomen

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Smooth death-feigning beetle — smooth-shelled relative of the warty ironclad (A. verrucosus). Blue-white waxy coating is a desert adaptation preventing water loss. Plays dead when disturbed — lies motionless for minutes. Cannot fly. Mojave and desert-edge habitats.
hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Desert specialist.

desc: Bioluminescent beetle with an extraordinary life history. Females are larviform (permanently larva-shaped) with glowing segments visible at night. Males are winged with enormous plumose antennae. Larvae prey on millipedes. One of LA's most remarkable beetles.

fm.Behavior: Bioluminescent females attract males

hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Beetle associate.

desc: Ivory-marked borer — brown longhorn with four ivory-white spots on each elytron. Famous for emerging from furniture, flooring, and structural wood years (even decades) after milling — larvae survive inside seasoned lumber. The longest recorded emergence gap is 40+ years.

fm.Behavior: Larvae survive in processed wood for years

hp: Wood/stem borer. Ivory-marked.

desc: Brilliant metallic green-and-gold carabid with purple/red elytral margins. Nocturnal caterpillar hunter that climbs trees to find prey. One of the most spectacular ground beetles in NA. Can spray hot defensive fluid. Beneficial pest control agent in forests.

fm.Behavior: Climbs trees for caterpillars; attracted to lights

hp: Active hunter — no web. Nocturnal. Preys on caterpillar.

desc: Large click beetle with prominent eyespots on pronotum. Western relative of A. oculatus. Click escape mechanism.

fm.Color: Black with white mottling; large eyespots

hp: Larvae in decaying wood — predatory on wood-boring grubs. Large eyespot pattern on pronotum.

desc: Sinuate flower longhorn — yellow-and-black banded cerambycid extremely common on flowers in chaparral and oak woodland. Adults visit buckwheat, yarrow, and other composites. Larvae develop over 2+ years in dead hardwood branches. One of the most frequently photographed longhorns in SoCal.
hp: Oak; buckwheat. Chaparral. Associated with Coast Live Oak and California Buckwheat.

desc: Hairy sand darkling — small rounded tenebrionid of coastal sand dunes. Declining severely with dune habitat loss across SoCal coastline. Nocturnal, burrowing in sand. One of several sand-obligate beetles threatened by beach development and grooming.
hp: Sand dune darkling beetle — declining with dune habitat loss. Indicator species for intact coastal dune ecosystems (Doyen 1976, Entomol News).

desc: Fat cactus longhorn — stouter and heavier-bodied than M. appressum. Desert specialist on chollas (Cylindropuntia). Flightless with fused elytra. Larvae bore in cholla stems. Distinguished from other Moneilema by thicker proportions. Mojave fringe habitats.
hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Cactus Host.

desc: Apple buprestid — dark bronze-brown jewel beetle. Major pest of fruit and shade trees — larvae bore serpentine galleries under bark, girdling branches. Prefers stressed or newly transplanted trees. Similar to C. femorata but darker. Important orchard pest in California.
hp: Under bark — wood-boring community. On apple.

desc: Brilliant metallic green with pale spots. European species occasionally intercepted in imported goods. Extremely fast predator of open ground. If established, would compete with native tiger beetles. Large eyes provide excellent binocular vision for prey tracking.

fm.Color: Bright metallic green with cream spots

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Beetle associate.

desc: Devastating invasive bark beetle spreading through LA's urban forest since 2012. Farms Fusarium fungus inside tree trunks. Over 60 tree species affected.

fm.Damage: Farms Fusarium fungus inside trees; kills host

hp: Polyphagous shot hole borer — ambrosia beetle farming Fusarium fungus in tunnel walls. Killing urban trees across SoCal. Invasive from SE Asia. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Invasive jewel beetle killing oaks. Larvae bore D-shaped galleries under bark. Spreading toward LA from San Diego.

fm.Color: Dark green-black with gold spots on elytra
fm.Damage: D-shaped exit holes; canopy dieback; tree death

hp: Gall-inducer on coast live oak. On Quercus (oak).

desc: Striking large beach-dwelling rove beetle. Sand-colored with dark mottling — superb camouflage on wet sand. Nocturnal predator on beach hoppers. California endemic.

fm.Color: Sand with dark mottling; flattened body

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Nocturnal. Superb camouflage on wet sand. Nocturnal predator on beach h.

desc: Yellow-orange with black stripes. Body produces cantharidin — causes blisters on skin contact. Larvae parasitize grasshopper eggs (beneficial). Adults eat flowers.

fm.Warning: Produces cantharidin — causes skin blisters on contact
fm.Habitat: Fields, gardens — adults on flowers; larvae parasitize grasshopper eggs

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Preys on grasshopper.

desc: Large, brilliantly colored blister beetle — black with bold yellow-orange cross pattern. Desert edge. Larvae parasitize solitary bee nests. Cantharidin producer.

fm.Color: Black with bold yellow-orange iron-cross pattern
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy areas — spring emergence

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Large species. Meloidae.

desc: Broad flat beetle with prominent elytral ridges. Found at carrion and decaying mushrooms. An important decomposer. Mites hitchhike on adults (phoresy).

fm.Color: Dark with reddish-brown pronotum; ridged elytra

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). On carrion.

desc: Metallic blue-green rove beetle common at carrion and compost. One of the most frequently encountered rove beetles in urban LA.
hp: On carrion — essential decomposer.

desc: Tiny metallic blue-green jumping beetle. 'Flea' refers to powerful jumping ability. Larvae and adults create shot-hole damage on grape and alder leaves.

fm.Habitat: Grape, alder, willows — shot-hole leaf damage
fm.Behavior: Powerful jumping ability like a flea

hp: On alder (alnus) in riparian habitat. Metallic coloring.

desc: Brown longhorn with tan zigzag bands. Common on recently killed oaks. Emerges from firewood — often the beetle crawling out of your fireplace woodpile.

fm.Habitat: Dead oak wood — emerges from firewood

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). On oaks (Quercus). Nautical.

desc: Metallic green-bronze jewel beetle. Larvae bore in incense cedar, juniper. Adults bask on sun-heated bark. Iridescent coloring makes them gems of the insect world.

fm.Habitat: Cedar, juniper forest — adults on sun-heated bark

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Wood/stem borer. Metallic coloring.

desc: Large shiny black ground beetle common under debris in gardens. Nocturnal predator and seed-eater. Attracted to lights. One of the most common large beetles in urban LA.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Large longhorn beetle devastating drought-stressed eucalyptus since 1984. Larvae bore under bark. Biocontrol by parasitoid wasp Avetianella longoi partially successful.

fm.Color: Dark brown with pale zigzag markings; very long antennae

hp: Biocontrol agent; eucalyptus; parasitoid of bees. Associated with Eucalyptus.

desc: One of the largest aquatic beetles in North America (up to 1.5 in). Swims using alternate leg strokes (vs. synchronized in diving beetles). Adults attracted to lights.

fm.Color: Dark olive-black; streamlined; keel on underside
fm.Habitat: Ponds, streams — attracted to lights at night
fm.Behavior: Alternate leg swimming (vs. synchronized in Dytiscidae)

hp: Giant water scavenger — largest NA water beetle (40mm; Young 1954). Aquatic predator of tadpoles and snails. Breathes via plastron.

desc: Small shiny black beetle with truncated elytra (abdomen exposed). Predator of fly larvae in carrion and dung. Important forensic entomology indicator.

fm.Color: Shiny jet black; short elytra; compact oval
fm.Habitat: Carrion, dung, compost — predatory on fly larvae

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Beetle associate.

desc: Tiny brown beetle whose larvae reduce hardwood to powder. Major pest of hardwood flooring, furniture, and lumber. Fine powder ('frass') falling from tiny exit holes diagnostic.

fm.Damage: Larvae reduce wood to powder; tiny exit holes + frass

hp: Larvae bore through hardwood — reduces flooring and furniture to powder. Major structural pest.

desc: Large orange-and-black carrion beetle. Buries small animal carcasses underground to provision larvae. Both parents provide extended biparental care — unusual for beetles.

fm.Color: Black with orange-red markings; large clubbed antennae
fm.Habitat: Open areas — buries small animal carcasses
fm.Behavior: Biparental care; parents feed larvae on carrion

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. On carrion.

desc: Small dung beetle — ♂ has two curved horns used in combat for mates. Introduced from Mediterranean. Tunnels beneath dung pats; important nutrient recycler.

fm.Color: Dark bronze-brown; ♂ with two curved horns
fm.Habitat: Pastures, open areas — tunnels beneath dung
fm.Behavior: ♂ horns for mate combat; important nutrient recycler

hp: On dung — nutrient recycler and parasite control. Introduced species.

desc: Heavy reddish-brown beetle attracted to lights en masse on warm spring nights. 'May beetles' or 'June bugs.' Larvae (white grubs) damage turf. 3-year life cycle.

fm.Habitat: Urban — attracted to lights; larvae in turf

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Scarabaeidae.

desc: Tiny ant-like beetle found on flowers. Actually predatory on small insects and mites. Narrow waist resembles ant. Very common but easily overlooked.

fm.Habitat: Flowers, leaf litter — predatory on small arthropods

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Very small species.

desc: Small humpbacked beetle that tumbles rapidly when disturbed — somersaults off flowers. Pointed abdomen projects beyond wing tips. Larvae bore in plant stems.

fm.Behavior: Rapid tumbling/spinning escape behavior

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Ambushes prey on flowers. Ant associate.

desc: Tiny beetle that develops entirely inside dried beans and peas. Major stored-product pest. Adults emerge from round exit holes in infested legumes. Cosmopolitan.

fm.Color: Mottled brown with pale elytra patches
fm.Habitat: Stored dried beans, peas — larvae develop inside single seed

hp: Stored product pest of dried foods and grain. Associated with Legumes.

desc: Large dark rhinoceros beetle — ♂ has three horns on pronotum. Larvae in decaying palm stumps. Attracted to lights. One of the most impressive beetles in SoCal.

fm.Habitat: Decaying palm stumps — attracted to lights

hp: Larvae (grubs) feed on decaying wood underground. Males have three pronotal horns. Attracted to lights.

desc: Small mottled bark-patterned beetle with broad snout. Found on dead wood with fungal growth. Larvae feed in fungi and decaying wood. Cryptic — resembles a bit of bark.

fm.Color: Mottled brown/gray bark pattern; broad flat snout

hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Under bark.

desc: Yellow mealworm beetle — dark brown tenebrionid whose larvae ('mealworms') are the most commercially important feeder insect worldwide. Sold for pet reptile/bird food. Adults in stored grain. Recently explored as sustainable human protein source. Model organism in physiology.
hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: Small brown chrysomelid on grape. Larvae feed on roots; adults skeletonize leaves and damage berry clusters. Important vineyard pest. Can be confused with other small brown beetles but association with grape and rootworm larval damage is diagnostic.
hp: Viticulture associate — on grapevines (Vitis). Vineyard pest or resident.

desc: Imported willow leaf beetle — tiny metallic blue-green chrysomelid. Dense colonies skeletonize willow leaves, sometimes causing complete defoliation along riparian corridors. Introduced from Europe. Very common along LA County's willow-lined streams and rivers.
hp: Foliage feeder on willows. Associated with Red Willow.

desc: Swamp milkweed beetle — large blue-black and orange chrysomelid on milkweed. Sequesters cardiac glycosides from host plant, making it toxic to predators. Bold orange-and-blue aposematic coloring warns birds. Both larvae and adults feed on milkweed leaves.
hp: On Asclepias (milkweed) — sequesters cardiac glycosides (Duffey & Scudder 1972, J Insect Physiol). Aposematic orange-and-blue. Chemical defense.

desc: Red with black spots. Specialist on milkweed. Sequesters toxins. Eyes split around antennae bases (Tetraopes = 'four eyes').

fm.Color: Red with black spots; eyes split by antennae

hp: On milkweed — sequesters cardiac glycosides. On Asclepias (milkweed).

desc: Large (25mm) mottled brown longhorn with very long antennae (2x body in males). Larvae bore large galleries in dead pines. White scutellum diagnostic. Can vector pine wilt nematode. Strong audible stridulation when handled.

fm.Color: Mottled brown; white scutellum; very long antennae

hp: In pine bark/wood — decomposer. Associated with Coulter Pine. On Pinus (pine). Large (25mm).

desc: Bright yellow-and-black wasp-mimic longhorn on goldenrod flowers in fall. Larvae bore black locust. Spectacular.

fm.Color: Black with bright yellow zigzag bands — wasp mimic

hp: Wasp mimic — Batesian mimicry with warning colors.

desc: The insect that changed American history — devastated Cotton Belt agriculture. Long curved snout. Eradicated from most of US but occasionally detected.

fm.History: Devastated US cotton; changed southern agriculture

hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares. Curculionidae.

hp: Larvae in dead wood — decomposer. Wood/stem borer. Ptinidae.

desc: Small ptinid with ornate pattern. Larvae in dead wood with fungal decay. Adults at lights.
hp: Larvae in dead wood — decomposer. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Tiny reddish cerambycid. In small dead branches and twigs. One of the smaller longhorn beetles.

fm.Color: Reddish-brown; very small for a longhorn

hp: Longhorn beetle — larvae bore in dead wood. Adults visit flowers. Small species.

desc: Medium scarab introduced from Africa for cattle dung biocontrol. Tunnels under dung pats. Important for pasture health.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). On dung.

desc: Small metallic buprestid named for Mariposa County. On chaparral flowers. Part of the diverse California jewel beetle fauna.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Chaparral habitat. Metallic. Family Buprestidae. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Small, unusually elongated tenebrionid of desert scrub. Under rocks and dead yucca leaves by day, active at night. Darker and more slender than most desert darklings. Part of the highly diverse tenebrionid fauna of SoCal deserts.
hp: Specialist on yucca (yucca spp.). Desert specialist.

desc: Large dark scarab emerging during winter rains. Males fly at dusk seeking wingless females. Larvae spend 8-13 years underground. Remarkable biology.

fm.Behavior: Flies during rain; larvae up to 13 years underground

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Scarabaeidae.

desc: Small blue-black chrysomelid with yellow elytral margins. Pest of crucifers — especially mustard greens and turnips. Introduced from South America.
hp: On brassicaceae (mustard family). Agricultural pest.

desc: Larva is the 'water penny' — flat round suction-cup-shaped larva clinging to rocks in streams. Important water quality indicator. Adults small, terrestrial.

fm.Color: Brown adult; larva is flat round disc on stream rocks

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Flattened body.

desc: Tiny aquatic weevil. Lives on aquatic plants. Breathes via air bubble trapped under elytra. One of few truly aquatic beetles.
hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Tiny. Tiny aquatic weevil. Lives on aquatic plants. Breathes via a.

desc: Small dark elaterid with four pale spots. Larvae in decaying wood. Click mechanism for righting.
hp: Predatory wireworm larvae.

desc: Small pale broad-nosed weevil that feeds on leaves of various native shrubs. Flightless — disperses only by walking. Nocturnal. Found in chaparral and coastal sage scrub. The flightless habit makes populations genetically isolated and potentially conservation-relevant.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. In chaparral habitat. Flightless.

desc: Small metallic buprestid visiting chaparral flowers. Larvae in dead branches. Part of the diverse Acmaeodera fauna of California.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Chaparral habitat. Metallic. Family Buprestidae. Small metallic buprestid visiting chaparral flowers.

desc: Medium dark tenebrionid with smooth featureless elytra (hence 'obsoleta' — lacking ornamentation). Desert and foothill habitats. Nocturnal. Under rocks. Part of the genus Stenomorpha — elongated, smooth-bodied desert darklings. SoCal near-endemic.
hp: Desert darkling — detritivore under rocks. Iconic desert beetle. Desert specialist.

desc: Small weevil living in beach wrack on seaweed. One of the few beetles adapted to the marine strand environment. Feeds on decaying kelp.
hp: Kelp curculio — flightless weevil in decomposing kelp wrack on beaches. Feeds on decaying seaweed. Coastal strand specialist.

desc: Small colorful clerid. Predatory — adults hunt bark beetles. Named for Santa Catalina Island.
hp: Bark beetle predator — important forest biocontrol. Preys on bark beetle. Family Cleridae. Small colorful clerid.

desc: Medium Eleodes related to other common species. Classic headstand defense. Desert and foothill trails.
hp: Desert detritivore — nocturnal under rocks. Family Tenebrionidae. Desert specialist. Cousin.

desc: Small dark tenebrionid with fine elytral lines. Very common in sandy soils and agriculture. Under debris.
hp: Very common in sandy coastal soils. Detritivore. Lined darkling of beaches and dunes.

desc: Small gray flightless weevil. Nocturnal leaf feeder. Notches leaf margins. On various shrubs.
hp: Nocturnal — attracted to lights.

desc: Tiny rounded beetle. Larvae feed on dried animal material — skins, feathers, museum specimens. Important museum pest.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Ant associate.

desc: Tiny flat beetle under bark. Predatory on other bark insects. Named for pioneer entomologist J.L. LeConte.
hp: Bark beetle predator — important forest biocontrol. Preys on bark beetle. Tiny flat beetle under bark. Predatory on other bark insects.

desc: Tiny round dermestid. Larvae damage woolens and museum specimens. Adults on flowers (feed on pollen).
hp: Dermestid — adults visit flowers for pollen; larvae on dried animal products.

desc: Medium buprestid with lined elytra. Larvae in dead branches of various trees. Can emerge from firewood.
hp: Larvae in dead wood — decomposer. Wood/stem borer. Flattened body.

desc: Medium dark staphylinid under bark and in leaf litter. Flat body adapted for bark crevice habitat. Predatory on small invertebrates. Fast runner. Like all rove beetles, short elytra expose flexible abdominal segments.
hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Predatory.

desc: Small dark cerambycid named for George Henry Horn, one of the most prolific 19th-century American entomologists. In dead hardwood branches. Nocturnal. Part of the diverse chaparral cerambycid fauna. Horn described over 1,600 beetle species.
hp: Nocturnal. Hardwood host.

desc: Medium brown cerambycid of mountain conifer forests. Larvae in dead pine and fir. Adults at lights.
hp: In dead pine wood. Associated with Coulter Pine. On Pinus (pine).

desc: Tiny rove beetle living in ant nests. Myrmecophile — produces chemicals that appease host ants. Fascinating social parasite.
hp: Parasitoid of solitary bee/wasp nests. In ant nests.

desc: Elongated meloeid with long curved mouthparts for nectar feeding. On flowers. Larvae parasitize solitary bee nests.

fm.Color: Dark; long curved mouthparts for nectar

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Beetle associate. Parasitoid.
